question: Based on Figure 5.1-2, does the NuScale RCS flow path appear to provide adequate driving head for natural circulation?

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design prioritizes maintaining coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support plant safety during normal and abnormal conditions. Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains its own RCS, and the design bases focus on reliable heat removal and chemical control.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across different power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring efficient operation, safety margins, and reliable natural circulation with minimal pressure losses in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the inspection, shipment, installation, and surveillance requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards for quality assurance and material integrity. It specifies procedures for maintaining a dry, controlled environment during shipment and details where further information on inspections, operating conditions, and fasteners can be found. Key references include the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, ASME NQA-1 quality assurance standards, and ASTM practices for material testing.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the Reactor Coolant System’s DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for safety and operability. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and that comprehensive testing—including position verification and in-situ heat removal tests—are conducted according to technical specifications and industry standards.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the use of low alloy steel, various grades of stainless steel, and nickel-based alloys, all in accordance with relevant BPVC Section II material specifications, to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and components, and is constructed and certified according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity throughout the plant’s design life. Material specifications for the RPV and its components are selected to maintain safety and durability, as detailed in referenced tables and standards.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the methodology used to calculate these limits, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the resulting pressure-temperature curves and data to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

edges.description: Section A provides a general overview of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation for coolant flow, while Section B specifies system features (like degasification and pressure boundary integrity) that support the RCS's ability to maintain proper flow and pressure, both contributing information relevant to assessing the adequacy of the driving head for natural circulation.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B evaluates the system's performance, including whether the RCS flow path provides adequate driving head for natural circulation, thus Section B assesses the adequacy of the design described in Section A.

Section A provides a general description of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details inspection and handling requirements for the reactor pressure vessel, which is a component of the RCS described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation for coolant flow, while Section B specifies system features (like degasification and pressure-retaining components) that support the RCS's ability to maintain adequate driving head for natural circulation.

Section A describes the design reliance on natural circulation for RCS flow, while Section B evaluates system performance criteria (including flow parameters) to ensure the design (from Section A) provides adequate driving head for natural circulation.

Section A describes the physical features of the RCS flow path, while Section B evaluates whether these features (including flow paths and parameters) meet performance criteria such as providing adequate driving head for natural circulation.

Section B provides a general overview of the NuScale RCS's reliance on natural circulation, while Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for components (such as DHRS valves) that are part of the RCS flow path described in Section B.

Section B describes the design basis of the NuScale RCS, emphasizing reliance on natural circulation, while Section A details inspection and classification requirements for components (such as DHRS valves) that are part of the RCS and its safety systems, thus providing implementation details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B describes the RCS's degasification and venting features, both referencing the presence and function of vents/valves in the RCS but without directly connecting to the analysis question about natural circulation driving head.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and its low-resistance flow path to the RCS, which supports the summary in Section B that the RCS relies on natural circulation without pumps, indicating that the pressurizer design facilitates the necessary driving head for natural circulation.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and flow path within the RCS, which supports the design basis in Section B that relies on natural circulation flow without pumps, connecting the pressurizer's function to the overall adequacy of the RCS flow path.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and its low-resistance flow path to the RCS, while Section B outlines the RCS's features for pressure control and gas removal, both providing information on how the RCS and pressurizer design facilitate pressure communication and flow necessary for natural circulation.

Section A provides detailed component and material information about the pressurizer, which is one of the RCS components generally described in Section B's summary of the RCS and its reliance on natural circulation.

Section A provides detailed component descriptions of the pressurizer, which is one of the RCS elements generally described in Section B's summary of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation.
Section A specifies the detailed components and materials of the pressurizer, which is a subsystem described more generally in Section B's summary of the RCS and its functions.

Section A describes the DHRS's reliance on the RCS to remove decay heat via natural circulation, while Section B outlines the RCS's design to provide natural circulation flow, directly supporting the DHRS's function.

Section A describes the DHRS's reliance on the RCS to remove decay heat via natural circulation, while Section B explains that the RCS is specifically designed to provide natural circulation flow without pumps, indicating that the RCS flow path is intended to provide the necessary driving head for systems like the DHRS.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in removing heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B provides a summary of the RCS's features and functions, with both sections connected by their focus on the RCS's ability to support safe heat removal and system integrity.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the RCS flow path, while Section B outlines the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System that relies on the RCS flow path to remove decay heat, thus Section A provides specific details that support the general system function described in Section B.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as a component of the RCS, while Section B provides a summary of the RCS, explicitly stating that it relies on natural circulation and includes the reactor vessel, thus connecting the vessel's role to the system's natural circulation function.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor vessel as part of the RCS, while Section B outlines the design basis that the RCS—including the reactor vessel—relies on natural circulation flow, connecting the vessel's configuration to the requirement for adequate driving head.

Section A describes the physical configuration and components of the NuScale reactor vessel as part of the RCS, while Section B provides a functional overview of the RCS, including its pressure-retaining features and degasification capabilities, thereby specifying how the vessel's design (A) supports the system's operational requirements (B) for maintaining adequate driving head for natural circulation.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its role in the reactor coolant system, while Section B specifies detailed requirements and analyses related to the reactor vessel's structural integrity and attachments, thus Section B provides details for Section A's general description.
